verses.text: And the LORD visited Sarah as He had said, and did unto her as He had spoken. The promise that hung over twenty-five years now happens in two simple verbs: visited, did. Isaac is conceived.
And the LORD visited Sarah as He had said, and did unto her as He had spoken. The promise that hung over twenty-five years now happens in two simple verbs: visited, did. Isaac is conceived.
Abraham names him Isaac — Hebrew for he laughs — the name God Himself chose. At eight days old, Isaac is circumcised, becoming the first to bear the sign of the covenant from his earliest days.
Abraham names him Isaac — Hebrew for he laughs — the name God Himself chose. At eight days old, Isaac is circumcised, becoming the first to bear the sign of the covenant from his earliest days.
Abraham was a hundred years old when Isaac was born. The math is impossible. Genesis records it twice in this chapter as if to make sure no one mistakes this for an ordinary event.

Sarah's laugh changes. Once it was the disbelieving giggle of chapter 18; now it is the open laughter of fulfillment. God hath made me to laugh, she says — all who hear will laugh with me. Who would have said Sarah should nurse children? But here he is.

Sarah's laugh changes. Once it was the disbelieving giggle of chapter 18; now it is the open laughter of fulfillment. God hath made me to laugh, she says — all who hear will laugh with me. Who would have said Sarah should nurse children? But here he is.
The day Isaac is weaned — about three years old — Abraham throws a great feast. The promised son has made it past the dangerous early years. The community celebrates with them.
But at the feast Sarah sees Ishmael mocking. Cast out this bondwoman and her son, she says. He shall not be heir with my son. Old jealousies harden into hard words.
But at the feast Sarah sees Ishmael mocking. Cast out this bondwoman and her son, she says. He shall not be heir with my son. Old jealousies harden into hard words.

Abraham is deeply grieved — Ishmael is also his son. God tells him: listen to Sarah; the promise runs through Isaac. But Ishmael will not be forgotten — I will make a nation of him too, because he is your seed. Grief and hope held together.

Abraham is deeply grieved — Ishmael is also his son. God tells him: listen to Sarah; the promise runs through Isaac. But Ishmael will not be forgotten — I will make a nation of him too, because he is your seed. Grief and hope held together.

Abraham is deeply grieved — Ishmael is also his son. God tells him: listen to Sarah; the promise runs through Isaac. But Ishmael will not be forgotten — I will make a nation of him too, because he is your seed. Grief and hope held together.

Early next morning, Abraham puts bread and a skin of water on Hagar's shoulder and sends her away. She wanders in the wilderness of Beersheba. The water runs out. She lays the boy under a shrub and walks a bowshot away, weeping. I cannot watch the child die.

Early next morning, Abraham puts bread and a skin of water on Hagar's shoulder and sends her away. She wanders in the wilderness of Beersheba. The water runs out. She lays the boy under a shrub and walks a bowshot away, weeping. I cannot watch the child die.

Early next morning, Abraham puts bread and a skin of water on Hagar's shoulder and sends her away. She wanders in the wilderness of Beersheba. The water runs out. She lays the boy under a shrub and walks a bowshot away, weeping. I cannot watch the child die.

But God hears the boy's voice. The angel of God calls from heaven: what aileth thee, Hagar? Fear not — God has heard the voice of the lad where he is. Arise, lift him up. I will make him a great nation. God answers the boy's cries while honoring his mother by name.

But God hears the boy's voice. The angel of God calls from heaven: what aileth thee, Hagar? Fear not — God has heard the voice of the lad where he is. Arise, lift him up. I will make him a great nation. God answers the boy's cries while honoring his mother by name.
Then God opens Hagar's eyes, and she sees a well of water — water that had been there all along. Sometimes deliverance is already in front of us; we just need eyes to see it.

God was with the boy. He grew up in the wilderness of Paran and became an archer. His mother — still acting for him, even in exile — finds him a wife from Egypt, her own homeland. Ishmael's line begins.

God was with the boy. He grew up in the wilderness of Paran and became an archer. His mother — still acting for him, even in exile — finds him a wife from Egypt, her own homeland. Ishmael's line begins.

Around that same time, Abimelech and his commander come to Abraham. God is with thee in all you do, they say. Swear to deal kindly with us. Abraham swears. The man God has blessed is now sought out by his neighbors.

Around that same time, Abimelech and his commander come to Abraham. God is with thee in all you do, they say. Swear to deal kindly with us. Abraham swears. The man God has blessed is now sought out by his neighbors.

Around that same time, Abimelech and his commander come to Abraham. God is with thee in all you do, they say. Swear to deal kindly with us. Abraham swears. The man God has blessed is now sought out by his neighbors.
Abraham brings up a grievance — Abimelech's servants had seized a well. Abimelech insists he knew nothing of it. Honest conflict is being handled honestly, not buried.
Abraham brings up a grievance — Abimelech's servants had seized a well. Abimelech insists he knew nothing of it. Honest conflict is being handled honestly, not buried.
Abraham gives Abimelech sheep and oxen, and the two cut a covenant. Then Abraham sets seven ewe lambs apart by themselves — an unusual gesture that catches Abimelech's notice.
Abraham gives Abimelech sheep and oxen, and the two cut a covenant. Then Abraham sets seven ewe lambs apart by themselves — an unusual gesture that catches Abimelech's notice.

Abimelech asks: what are these seven lambs? They are a witness, says Abraham, that I dug this well. The place is named Beersheba — well of the oath, or well of the seven. The land is being claimed peacefully now.

Abimelech asks: what are these seven lambs? They are a witness, says Abraham, that I dug this well. The place is named Beersheba — well of the oath, or well of the seven. The land is being claimed peacefully now.

Abimelech asks: what are these seven lambs? They are a witness, says Abraham, that I dug this well. The place is named Beersheba — well of the oath, or well of the seven. The land is being claimed peacefully now.

After the covenant, Abimelech returns home. Abraham plants a tamarisk tree in Beersheba and calls there on the name of the LORD, the everlasting God. He stays in Philistine territory many days — settled, but still a sojourner.

After the covenant, Abimelech returns home. Abraham plants a tamarisk tree in Beersheba and calls there on the name of the LORD, the everlasting God. He stays in Philistine territory many days — settled, but still a sojourner.
